next_steps: Prospect to download and begin self-service trial via HubSpot Marketplace
Bryan to organize prospect's requirements list into prioritized onboarding hit-list with starter Loom videos
Follow-up call scheduled for Thursday next week (May 7) at 4:10 PM CET
Prospect to attempt bringing manager to follow-up call
Phase 1 focus: pricing, price books, rules, approvals. Phase 2: document generation and templates

jtbd.job: Replace disconnected quoting and e-signature workflow with a unified CPQ inside HubSpot
jtbd.context: Currently using DocuSign for signatures and HubSpot for CRM, but quoting lives outside HubSpot. Reps manually input line items after quotes are signed. 200+ quoters across 20+ language markets.
jtbd.desired_outcome: Single tool that handles pricing, quoting, approvals, document generation, and e-signature natively within HubSpot, eliminating dual data entry and disconnected workflows

jtbd.job: Enforce discount governance and approval workflows without manual review of every quote
jtbd.context: HubSpot's native pricing is per-unit only. Tiered pricing requires workarounds with multiple units. No way to set discount thresholds that trigger approvals automatically.
jtbd.desired_outcome: Set price discounts per product (not just per unit), track total discounts, and auto-route quotes exceeding thresholds to approvers

jtbd.job: Manage 78+ quote templates across 20+ languages without overwhelming individual reps
jtbd.context: 78 templates exist today. A single salesperson only needs about 4. German reps should not see Italian contracts. No template scoping or role-based visibility exists in current setup.
jtbd.desired_outcome: Template visibility scoped by user group or region so reps see only the templates relevant to their market

pains.pain: HubSpot native pricing only supports per-unit discounting, making tiered pricing and set-price discounts impossible without workarounds
pains.trigger: Every quote involving tiered pricing or custom discount amounts. Reps must create multiple line items to simulate tiers, then a percentage discount applies to all units instead of the specific tier.
pains.cost: Quoting errors, rework, and slow turnaround on complex deals. Reps cannot simply enter a set discount price per product.
pains.urgency_reason: Core quoting workflow is broken for their pricing model. This is the primary driver of the CPQ evaluation.

pains.pain: No template scoping: 78 templates visible to all reps when each rep needs only 4
pains.trigger: Every time a rep creates a quote and must scroll through 78 templates to find the right one. German reps see Italian contracts. No way to restrict visibility by user group.
pains.cost: Rep confusion, slower quote creation, risk of using wrong template for wrong market. Multiplied across 200+ quoters in 20+ language markets.
pains.urgency_reason: Scale of 200 quoters across 20+ languages makes this a daily operational drag

pains.pain: No discount tracking or reporting on per-product and total discount amounts
pains.trigger: Finance and management cannot see discount patterns across quotes. No visibility into what discounts are being given per product or in aggregate.
pains.cost: Potential margin erosion invisible until revenue is recognized. No data to inform pricing strategy or identify discounting abuse.
pains.urgency_reason: Connected to approval workflow requirement but not yet causing visible revenue losses that have been quantified

pains.pain: No approval workflows tied to discount thresholds
pains.trigger: Discounts exceed acceptable levels with no automatic escalation. Manual review is the only control mechanism.
pains.cost: Either every quote gets manually reviewed (bottleneck) or discounts go unchecked (margin risk).
pains.urgency_reason: Prospect explicitly connected discount tracking to approvals as a core requirement

pains.pain: Disconnected quoting and CRM: line items must be manually entered into HubSpot after DocuSign-based quotes
pains.trigger: Every closed deal. After a quote is signed via DocuSign, someone must go back and enter line items into HubSpot deals manually.
pains.cost: Double data entry across 200+ quoters. Reporting on quoted products and pricing is unreliable because it depends on manual re-entry. Time lost per deal on administrative work.
pains.urgency_reason: Quotivity rep surfaced this as a key value driver and prospect confirmed the pain

pains.pain: DealHub was too complex: required a dedicated admin and extensive implementation
pains.trigger: Evaluated DealHub through a peer's demo. Learned that the reference company has a dedicated DealHub admin just to maintain the tool.
pains.cost: Prospect dropped DealHub from evaluation. If a tool requires a dedicated admin, it is too heavy for their operational model.
pains.urgency_reason: This is an active disqualifier shaping the current evaluation. Self-service capability is a must-have.

pains.pain: Cannot lock sections of quotes (especially terms and conditions) to prevent buyer edits
pains.trigger: Sending quotes with legal terms that buyers could modify before signing.
pains.cost: Legal and compliance risk if terms are altered. Requires manual verification of every signed quote.
pains.urgency_reason: Mentioned as a requirement but not described as causing active incidents

pains.pain: E-signature workflow gaps: need signing order, multiple signees, reassignment, verification steps, and recall/edit capability
pains.trigger: Complex signature routing across multiple stakeholders. Some customers still require wet signatures. Need email/phone verification before signing.
pains.cost: Current DocuSign workflow handles these but moving to a new tool risks losing these capabilities.
pains.urgency_reason: Bryan acknowledged some DocuSign-like features are not available in Quotivity. Prospect may need to accept trade-offs.

workflow_reality.current_process: Quoting and document generation handled via DocuSign, disconnected from HubSpot CRM. Reps build quotes outside HubSpot, route for signature via DocuSign, then manually enter line items back into HubSpot deals. 78 quote templates managed across 20+ languages. Pricing and discount calculations done manually or via HubSpot's limited per-unit model.
brittleness_indicators: Line items manually re-entered into HubSpot after quotes are signed — data integrity depends on manual discipline across 200 quoters
78 templates with no scoping — any rep can select any template including wrong-market contracts
Tiered pricing requires workaround of adding multiple line items to simulate tiers
No automated discount controls — governance is manual or absent
Prospect is building requirements in a PowerPoint deck — no formal evaluation framework exists internally
First-time platform buyer at this company — no established procurement process

evaluated.name: DealHub
evaluated.why_they_passed: Too complex. A peer company has a dedicated DealHub admin. Prospect wants a tool they can figure out on their own. If they cannot self-serve through the evaluation, the tool is too heavy for their team.
evaluated.key_quote: That's why we dropped DealHub, because I got a demo from friends and they basically have an admin just for DealHub, and that's not something that we are looking for.

evaluated.name: DocuSign
evaluated.what_they_liked: Handles signing order, multiple signees, reassignment, verification steps, document upload, recall and edit. Mature e-signature capabilities.
evaluated.why_they_are_evaluating_alternatives: DocuSign handles documents and signatures but is disconnected from HubSpot CRM. No pricing, quoting, or discount governance. Creates manual re-entry of line items.

current_workaround.description: DocuSign for e-signatures and document generation + HubSpot CRM for deal management + manual line item entry to bridge the two. 78 templates across 20+ languages with no access scoping.
current_workaround.adequacy: Functional for signatures but creates disconnected data, manual rework, no discount governance, and no quoting intelligence. The gap between document execution and CRM data is entirely manual.

quotes.quote: Currently in HubSpot, it's based on the units. So the price per unit. And then if the tier is, if we want to do tiered pricing, then we have to put multiple units, and then if they put in the discount of 10, then it discounts four units by 10. I would like to be able for them to just be able to put in set price as a discount per product.
quotes.usage_notes: Concrete articulation of HubSpot native CPQ's tiered pricing limitation. Directly maps to Quotivity's price book and discount capabilities. Strong for 'HubSpot CPQ limitations' content.

quotes.quote: I have around 78 templates right now, and as a salesperson, I only need four. I don't want German people to see the Italian contracts.
quotes.usage_notes: Quantifies template management pain at scale. 78 templates, 200 quoters, 20+ languages. Perfect for content about template governance in multi-market quoting operations.

quotes.quote: That's why we dropped DealHub, because I got a demo from friends and they basically have an admin just for DealHub, and that's not something that we are looking for.
quotes.usage_notes: Direct competitive intelligence against DealHub. Validates Quotivity's positioning on ease of use and self-service. Strong for DealHub comparison content and 'CPQ without a dedicated admin' messaging.

quotes.quote: The idea is that if we are also not able to figure it out on our own, then it might be a bit too complex for us to set up.
quotes.usage_notes: Reveals a key buying criterion: self-service trial ability is a proxy for implementation complexity. If the evaluator cannot build without hand-holding, the tool fails the test. Strong for product-led growth and ease-of-use content.

quotes.quote: Discount cannot go over certain price without an approval.
quotes.usage_notes: Clear statement of approval workflow requirement tied to discount thresholds. Connects to finance/controller persona pain point. Use in discount governance and approval workflow content.

quotes.quote: We really want to just test the trial. Get the hands on the tool, see what we can do, figure out on our own.
quotes.usage_notes: Signals product-led buying motion. Prospect resists structured sales-led evaluation. Useful for understanding RevOps buyer behavior and trial conversion strategy.

quotes.quote: I would like for customers to be able to write, populate that info in these custom properties.
quotes.usage_notes: Requirement for buyer-side data entry that writes back to HubSpot custom properties. Technical requirement for integration content.

value_language: figure it out on our own
too complex for us to set up
only display the stuff that's necessary

deviant_terms.meaning: A dedicated administrator whose role is solely maintaining the CPQ tool. Used as a disqualifying signal for tool complexity.
deviant_terms.frequency: used once but was the decisive factor in dropping DealHub

deviant_terms.meaning: Internal evaluation process where the evaluator and their manager send each other quotes and try to break or find limitations in the tool
deviant_terms.frequency: used once, describes their evaluation methodology

deviant_terms.meaning: Tool-specific quirks, edge cases, and limitations that only surface during hands-on usage, not demos
deviant_terms.frequency: used once, reveals preference for self-discovery over sales-guided evaluation

keyword_candidates.source: prospect needs discount thresholds that auto-trigger approval routing — not available in HubSpot native
